{"step_by_step_thinking": "Rheumatic heart disease (RHD) is a chronic condition resulting from prior rheumatic fever, which causes inflammation and damage to the heart valves. The key pathological feature in RHD is the involvement of the valve leaflets due to chronic inflammation. The lesions typically affect the valve cusps, which are the parts of the valve that directly interact with each other during valve closure. The inflammation leads to thickening, scarring, and fusion of the valve leaflets, particularly affecting the mitral and aortic valves. The UMLS documents specifically state that chronic rheumatic valvulitis and rheumatic heart valve stenosis are located in the heart valves (srdeční chlopně), and that rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is a structural disorder of the heart affecting the mitral valve. These pathologies are primarily due to damage to the valve cusps and their coaptation. While the free margins and line of closure may be involved, the primary site of lesion in RHD is the valve cusps, where the chronic inflammatory process causes thickening and fusion. Therefore, based on the anatomical and pathological understanding of RHD, the most accurate answer is the valve cusps.", "answer_choice": "C"}
